BitForge — four new Beginner-tier kits: ultrarealistic 8K image generation prompts
Replace placeholder PNGs (luma_cube, breeze_beacon, glow_sentinel, pixel_pal) after rendering.
Each numbered paragraph below is a single paste-ready prompt; target length ~500 words each (word counts are approximate—verify with wc -w on your copy if your tool requires an exact budget).

================================================================================
PROMPT 1 — LUMA CUBE (crafts / frosted RGB desk cube)
================================================================================
Create an ultrarealistic 8K studio product photograph of a DIY maker kit called “LumaCube” for a premium electronics marketplace. The hero subject is a compact desk lamp assembled as a perfect cube roughly twelve to fourteen centimeters per side, built from four vertical panels of semi-opaque frosted acrylic with subtle laser-etched seams and micro-beveled top edges; the cube sits on a thin matching frosted base ring that conceals a USB-C port on the rear face, oriented so the port is visible but not dominant. Inside the cube, a continuous RGB LED strip is diffused so the faces glow evenly with a soft vertical gradient from warm amber near the bottom to cool cyan near the top, suggesting programmable color without harsh LED hotspots or visible diode spacing. On the front-bottom edge of the base, integrate a minimal capacitive touch ring of brushed aluminum about twenty-five millimeters across, with a faint illuminated ring when the control is active. Include a secondary disassembled flat-lay in the foreground on the same sweep: lasercut acrylic panels with protective paper half-peeled to show the contrast between glossy film and satin frosted glass, a flexible LED strip partially unspooled from a small reel, a compact printed circuit board with JST connectors and silkscreen labels too small to read, a braided USB-C cable with authentic strain relief, a tiny hex key, and a single-page glossy instruction foldout showing exploded diagrams and a faint BitForge wordmark—no legible paragraphs, only schematic blocks and numbered callouts. The tabletop is matte white cyclorama with a gentle contact shadow and soft ambient occlusion where the base meets the surface; lighting follows a disciplined three-point softbox scheme with a large key from camera-left, restrained fill to preserve acrylic translucency, and a tight rim from behind-right to outline panel edges. Use a full-frame macro-product aesthetic with an eighty-five millimeter lens at f/5.6 for edge-to-edge sharpness at moderate distance, ISO one hundred, tripod-stabilized, perspective slightly above eye level, composition centered with rule-of-thirds offset so the flat-lay occupies the lower-right quadrant without crowding the hero cube. Materials must feel tangible: acrylic carries micro-scratches only where film was removed; aluminum is fingerprint-free with believable brushed directionality; cables show molded ends and subtle labeling wear. Extend the brief with post-production intent: neutral color grade with slightly lifted shadows, white balance near fifty-two hundred kelvin, restrained contrast so highlights on acrylic never clip to pure white, and no oversaturated neon cyberpunk palettes—keep the mood Apple-store minimal and calm. Document negatives explicitly for the model: reject cartoon rendering, CGI plastic sheen, floating holographic UI, readable marketing copy beyond faint branding, human hands, cluttered workshop debris, fisheye distortion, ugly lens flare blobs, low resolution, painterly brushstrokes, duplicated hero products, stock watermarks, cliché lens-flare stock tropes, exaggerated bloom that hides texture, visible LED pixel grids through the diffuser, or misaligned panel gaps. Specify output as eight-K photoreal commercial catalog frames with shallow but controlled depth of field: cube faces tack-sharp, instruction sheet in foreground slightly softer, noise-free shadows, and micro-contrast that rewards zooming without looking HDR-halated.

================================================================================
PROMPT 2 — BREEZE BEACON (eco / USB desk fan + temperature cue)
================================================================================
Produce an ultrarealistic eight-K hero image of the “BreezeBeacon” beginner kit: a quiet USB-powered desk fan designed for makers who are learning simple environmental sensing and threshold logic. The assembled unit should dominate the frame as a compact ninety-two millimeter PWM fan with seven soft-gray blades and a subtle hub logo too small to read, housed in a rounded-square frame fabricated from recycled paper–fiber composite that reads as speckled warm gray with visible fibers and a matte, almost paper-like tooth. The frame tilts about fifteen degrees rearward on a weighted base that includes a small maker-friendly cavity behind a snap-off service panel; depict the panel slightly ajar so viewers can see color-coded jumper wires, a miniature temperature sensor on a breakout board, and two through-hole indicator LEDs embedded in the front bezel—green for comfortable airflow temperature and amber when the ambient reading suggests warming—without turning the scene into a messy electronics bench. Route a braided USB-C cable from the rear with a right-angle connector that sits naturally against a desk edge. Spread the unboxed kit across a light birch plywood tabletop lit by soft daylight from a large off-camera window, producing gentle diagonal shadows near fifty-five hundred kelvin: include the fan module, two optional printed feet, four silicone anti-vibration pads, screws organized in a small kraft insert tray, a compact screwdriver, a reusable cable tie, and a slim recyclable cardboard sleeve standing in the background with abstract geometric eco patterns and only a tiny BitForge mark—no readable marketing paragraphs. Discuss lighting behavior: favor naturalistic bounce from a white card reflector, keep specular highlights on plastics soft and elongated rather than pin-sharp, and allow only the wood to carry faint dust motes for realism while the product stays pristine. Camera direction: fifty millimeter lens at f/4 for a natural perspective, eye-level three-quarter view that communicates blade curvature, base depth, and the tilt mechanism; maintain parallel verticals or correct perspective subtly so the product does not look like a phone snapshot. Expand the narrative mood as calm, sustainable, and trustworthy—Scandinavian workspace rather than gaming battlestation. For technical fidelity, insist on eight-K resolution, crisp microcontrast that reveals fiber texture in the composite frame, plausible PBR-like responses on ABS and polycarbonate fan housings, and global illumination that feels photographic rather than ray-traced plastic. Negative constraints: exclude industrial grime, rainbow RGB on blades, oversized gamer typography, people, pets, exaggerated outdoor wind with flying papers, motion blur on blades beyond a subtle operational hint, cheap ultra-gloss plastics, bokeh soap bubbles behind the product, cartoon illustration, low-poly meshes, readable warranty text, QR codes, fake heat shimmer, flames, or smoke. Close with a reminder that the final image must read as a premium marketplace listing suitable for a carousel tile: legible silhouette, balanced negative space, and honest eco materials. Add explicit aspect guidance: leave margin on left and right so carousel arrows and card padding never crop the hero; keep the fan silhouette readable at thumbnail scale. Mention that blade motion may be frozen nearly static with only a whisper of motion implied by micro blur at tips if needed, but default to crisp blades for catalog clarity.

================================================================================
PROMPT 3 — GLOW SENTINEL (sensor / PIR warm LED puck)
================================================================================
Render an ultrarealistic eight-K product scene for “Glow Sentinel,” a beginner motion-activated night light kit that teaches occupancy sensing without intimidation. The primary object is a puck-shaped LED fixture approximately eighty-five millimeters in diameter and twenty-eight millimeters tall, combining a matte white polycarbonate diffuser dome with a light-gray ABS back shell; include a discreet fresnel lens window for the passive infrared sensor facing the viewer, aligned straight and professional. Along the rim, show either micro-USB or USB-C charging under a small silicone plug, plus a flush rotary dimmer wheel that feels like high-volume consumer hardware rather than a prototype. When the device is illustrated in its active state, the diffuser should emit warm twenty-seven hundred kelvin light with believable subsurface scattering and no visible individual LED dies at normal viewing distance—only a creamy, even glow with gentle falloff at the dome equator. Offer two interpretable staging options within one cohesive brief: first, a believable domestic hallway vignette with pale plaster wall, slim oak trim, a narrow console, and a single soft shadow anchoring the puck; second, a pure high-key catalog arrangement on seamless white with an adjacent exploded layout showing the PIR module on a small PCB, battery holder or compact Li-ion pack, magnetic mounting plate, screws, adhesive pads on a release liner, and a pictographic quick-start card without readable sentences. Describe lighting setups distinctly: for catalog, a large overhead softbox with subtle paper gradient; for lifestyle, practical warm bounce from the fixture itself with exposure that preserves highlight rolloff. Camera recommendations: thirty-five millimeters at f/5.6 for the environmental frame to avoid cramped interiors; ninety millimeters macro for a second hero that reveals dome texture and mold parting lines. Manufacturing realism matters: show subtle draft angles, faint laser-marked certification icons that remain illegible, and mold seams that are present but not ugly. Expand negative prompts to steer away from horror red lighting, surveillance CCTV aesthetics, harsh blue cold LEDs, strobing police lights, creepy anthropomorphic shadows, blood, smeared fingerprints on the diffuser, cracked plastics, fisheye distortion, anime or illustration styles, duplicated PIR windows, spaghetti wiring, steampunk brass gears, comically oversized screws, sensor dust on lens glass, heavy vignetting, stock silhouettes of families, or readable legal disclaimers. Style lock: premium consumer electronics similar to modern smart-home lighting, restrained and trustworthy, eight-K, noise-free shadow detail, accurate warm-white color science, and highlights that never posterize. Add one more sensory cue: the hallway scene should feel like a real apartment width—roughly ninety centimeters of visible floorboard or runner—so scale reads human without showing people. Finish by requesting a composition that would sit comfortably beside other BitForge cards: centered product with breathing room, consistent scale, and no distracting props beyond what reinforces “safe night lighting for beginners.” Emphasize repeatability: the same product geometry and materials should match a hypothetical sibling SKU photo—consistent shadow length, consistent sweep curvature—so the home-page carousel feels like one art-directed session. Note that wall color should stay neutral greige or off-white so the warm LED does not fight saturated paint.

================================================================================
PROMPT 4 — PIXEL PAL (crafts / 8×8 LED matrix bamboo frame)
================================================================================
Generate an ultrarealistic eight-K marketplace hero image for “Pixel Pal,” an eight-by-eight addressable LED matrix kit presented in a sustainable bamboo desktop frame aimed at creative beginners. The frame should appear CNC-milled from solid bamboo ply with visible end grain on the short sides, gentle corner radii, and a subtle plant-oil finish that warms the wood without looking varnished-plastic. Recess the matrix slightly behind a matte black acrylic mask with square apertures registered precisely to each pixel cell; the LEDs should display a friendly low-resolution heart icon and a tiny animated arrow motif suggested by shifted glow patterns—soft bloom between cells yet a crisp underlying grid, with absolutely no readable alphanumeric text on the matrix. Along the bottom edge of the frame, place one tactile button with a brushed stainless cap and a miniature status LED that reads as a polished consumer detail. Surround the assembled piece with an honest unboxing spread: the flexible eight-by-eight panel resting on pink anti-static foam, a compact controller board with USB-C and modest connector height, a flat ribbon cable with correct orientation keys, four rubber feet, a mini folding stand leg, nylon screws in a tiny tray, and a cotton drawstring parts bag with a woven label too small to read. Stage on a soft gradient gray sweep tabletop with a faint, controlled reflection that anchors the object; light with a large diffused key from camera-left and a subtle colored gel bounce that picks up magenta-to-cyan hints on the sweep only, never polluting the bamboo colorimetry—wood must remain natural and photographic. Camera: seventy millimeter macro at f/eight so pixel apertures stay sharp across the plane, with focus-stacked clarity that does not look artificially oversharpened. Post direction: emphasize micro-detail in bamboo fibers, manage speculars on the acrylic mask without flattening the blacks, and keep LED bloom physically plausible. Negative list: block Minecraft voxel aesthetics, retro CRT scanlines, arcade cabinets, gaming chairs, mechanical keyboards dominating the frame, chaotic cable nests, soldering irons, fire, smoke, cartoon heart clip art, anime sparkles, rainbow anamorphic flares unless extremely subtle, duplicated bamboo frames, watermarks, HUD overlays, QR codes, readable source code, OLED text panels misrepresenting the matrix, sixteen-by-sixteen grids pretending to be eight-by-eight, warped perspective, plastic faux-wood grain, or bubbling veneer defects. Output specification: eight-K photoreal editorial suitable for Apple-style e-commerce, honest materials, believable scale—optionally include a discreet scale reference such as an SD card edge or coin only if it stays subtle and does not compete with the product. Conclude with a quality bar: the image must survive zoomed inspection in a horizontal carousel beside other kits, maintaining consistent lighting language and premium tactile credibility. Reinforce framing: keep the assembled Pixel Pal occupying roughly the upper two-thirds of the canvas so card overlays and “NEW” labels in the UI never obscure the LED pattern; scatter kit parts lower in the frame with clear separation. State explicitly that bamboo end grain must not show unnatural symmetry or cloned texture tiles—each side should differ slightly like real machined stock.

================================================================================
END — After rendering, overwrite luma_cube.png, breeze_beacon.png, glow_sentinel.png, pixel_pal.png in frontend/public/catalog/featured-kits/ and mirror copies under maker-platform/assets/featured-kits/. Reseed product-service and flush Redis cache key for products if your deployment caches listings.
